properties.title: 'Branding the Brazos' Sculptures
properties.description:
Artist: Robert Summers, installation completed 2014. 
This series of one and a half life-sized bronze sculptures depicts longhorns and riders, commemorating Waco’s history as the Brazos River crossing of the Chisholm Trail. The first sculptures were installed in 2008 and depict three vaqueros (cowboys) each representing a different cultural influence on Waco's past and present; Hispanic, African American, and European. The sculptures were commissioned by Cultural Arts of Waco, and funded through private donations led by Waco citizens Betsy and Clifton Robinson.

properties.title: 'Blood Feather' Sculpture
properties.description:
Texas granite. 2005
Sculptor: Rolando Saenz
“Blood Feather,” means living feather, suggesting transformation and renewal, and is a tribute to all the people of Waco. Blood Feather was exhibited at the Cultural Arts of Waco “Waco National Outdoor Sculpture Invitational” 2006 by Rolando Saenz Monumental Arts and Sculpture Studio, Waco, Texas. Acquired by the Greater Waco Chamber of Commerce Permanent Art Collection in 2008 and on public display outside the Chamber. For over 26 years Rolando has been taming the many different styles of granite and marble with his artistic talent. From small table top sculptures to towering monumental art.

properties.title: 'Divine Servant' Sculpture
properties.description: Artist: Max Greiner, 2015

properties.title: 'Las Mesas' Sculpture
properties.description:
Dakota granite, base Fredericksburg granite. 2002
Sculptor: Jesús Bautista Moroles 
Jesús Moroles , (1950-2015) International sculptor known for his abstract granite sculptures. He lived and worked in Rockport, Texas, where his studio and workshop were based, and where all of his work was prepared and finished before being shipped out for installation. “Las Mesas” was exhibited at the Cultural Arts of Waco “Waco National Outdoor Sculpture Invitational” 2005 and was donated to the city in July 2005. It can be viewed in front of the Waco Convention Center.

properties.title: Doris Miller Memorial
properties.description:
Architect: Stan Carroll, AIA: Stainless Steel & Concrete
Sculptor: Eddie Dixon: Bronze
Dedicated December 7th 2018, final completion 2020. Doris Miller was born and raised in Waco and was the first African-American to be awarded the Navy Cross due to his outstanding bravery during the attack on Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941. The “Many Sailors make a Ship” stainless steel ship’s hull honors the navy servicemen and women who lost their lives at Pearl Harbor, and those alongside Doris Miller when the aircraft carrier Liscomb Bay was torpedoed and sunk in 1943 at the Battle of the Gilbert Isles in the Pacific. The bronze image of Doris Miller, 150% of lifesize, “Coming Home” when manning the rail on the return to port.

properties.title: 'Florence, The Oaxcan Cow' Sculpture
properties.description:
Artist: Alethea Bateman, 2000
This brightly painted cow stands at St. Paul’s Episcopal Day School, that Bateman’s grandchildren attended. 

Florence was part of the "Wacows" fundraiser for Art Center of Waco in 2000 that saw 40 cows painted by local artists, given pun-worthy names, and auctioned. You can occasionally spot the cows around town in some unusual places. Let us know if you do!
Photo Credit Rod Aydelotte, Waco Trib, 2015.

properties.title: 'Friendship is for All Seasons' Sculpture
properties.description:
Artist: Eugenia Kamrath Mygdal, 2001
(from the marker) This sculpture is dedicated to the idea that a community of citizens thrives and is most able to accomplish its goals when the spirit of friendship pervades its daily life.

Children are used as the main subject of the sculpture because of their attitudes of fun, happiness, curiosity and innocence. They also represent the four major cultural groups of Waco's population.

The animals in the grouping are important reminders that human existence is a shared reality.

The lamb, together with the boy holding him, represents the farming and ranching industries that helped make Waco a prominent central Texas city.

Skipper, the dog, symbolizes faithfulness and watchfulness.

The turtle symbolizes the idea of eternity.

properties.title: 'Spadix' Sculpture
properties.description:
Bronze on granite base, 2001 
Sculptor: Mike Cunningham 
Acquired by the Greater Waco Chamber of Commerce Permanent Art Collection in 2008 and on public display outside the Chamber. Mike Cunningham. Argyle, Texas has taught and conducted workshops for many North Texas communities and has been a tenured professor and lecturer at the University of North Texas, School of Visual Arts. His sculptural works feature a variety of material including granite, bronze, stone, acrylic and wood.

properties.title: Waco Sculpture Zoo
properties.description:
Various Artists, January 2020. 
This series of 28 animal-based sculptures is by 17 leading artists from across the USA. Click the photo to discover interactive information about the project and each individual artwork or visit zoo.creativewaco.org

properties.title: Waco Tornado Kinetic Sculptures
properties.description:
Mark White, 2019
These sculptures commemorate the tornado of 1953 that devastated downtown Waco, and honor the heroic citizens who worked tirelessly to rebuild the city.
 
Donated by Clifton and Betsy Robinson in honor of the Waco High School graduating classes of 1953, 1954, 1955 and 1956, whose lives were forever shaped by this event.
 
Installed March 2019
With thanks to Clifton and Betsy Robinson, Roosevelt Tower, LLC, and the City of Waco

properties.title: 1000 Hopes for Waco Mural
properties.description:
Creative Waco ARTprenticeship, 2018
This artwork at 315 S. University Parks Drive was developed through Creative Waco’s ARTPrenticeship program. Aspiring young creatives from Waco ISD high schools earn real world concept-to-completion mentored work experience during the 8-week program. Under guidance from local artist mentors, the apprentices created a fresh and eye-catching work of art that reflects the beauty of our community.

The motif of cranes or birds taking flight is a symbol of hope and blessing in many cultures. The origami cranes are symbolic of how the creative skills that built our community are handed from one generation to the next, just as the skill of folding paper into a crane has been taught by one artist to the next for thousands of years.

This mural symbolizes Waco emerging from the chaos of tornado, storms, floods, and turbulent history, to gradually take form and flight towards a horizon of hope. The students talked and designed around themes of hope, courage, and emerging from a past that we can’t influence into a future that we can.

“1,000 Hopes for Waco” was created by artist mentors Will Suarez (lead designer), Cade Kegerreis, Megan Morris Major, and Sean Oswald. Apprentices were Naya Banda, Nadia Benett, Lizbeth Duarte -Tavera, Londell Gilmore, Charla Goosby, Magnolia Oliphant, Willie Padilla, Tamra Richie, Savana Salazar and Joseph Wlazlinski.

properties.title: A Message of Hope Mural
properties.description:
In partnership with The Cove, local artist Julie Cervantes and apprentices Breneisha Thompson, Kelle DeSouza, and Passion Salazar, designed and painted this original work for The Cove in 2020. Sharing the message of hope and how the power of community can help overcome obstacles, the bird represents our highest hopes for every Cove Scholar.

ARTPrenticeship was able to pilot a hybrid program in response to the COVID-19 pandemic that set precedent to resume a full-size program in the fall of 2020.

THIS PROJECT WAS CREATED BY:
Artist Mentors:

Julie Cervantes (lead designer)

ARTPrentices:

Kelle DeSouza, Breneisha Thompson and Passion Salazar

Project Manager:

Stefanie Wheat-Johnson

properties.title: Abstract Brush & Line Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Gabby Storey, 2022
A truly eye-catching mural set, these two garage doors feature splotches of color reminiscent of paintbrush strokes, wavy lines, circles, and clumps of shapes. The use of color in this piece is incredibly thoughtful and comes together to create a fun, exciting story.

properties.title: Abstract Mural
properties.description: Artist: Mick Burson, 2014

properties.title: Altogether Beautiful
properties.description:
Artist: Joe Rooster

Joe created this piece to represent the beauty of women. The bright colors represent all types of women from all walks of life. Joe wanted women to see this and to see themselves as beautiful strong people. Despite what they may have been through, they are beautiful in every way.

properties.title: Ambold's Mural
properties.description:
Tony Bryant, 2019
Images from Waco's past and present mingle on the side of Ambold's Key, Lock & Alarm building on Franklin Avenue. Since this photo was taken, the City of Waco logo on this mural has been revised.
Photo Credit, Jerry Larson, Waco Trib

properties.title: Bold Waco
properties.description:
Artist: Nick Frigo, 2017
Hole in the Roof designer, Nick Frigo worked on this mural in his free time.
Photo Credit, Rod Aydelotte, Waco Trib, 2017

properties.title: Cast Thy Burden
properties.description: This wall is about to become part of "ARTPLACE", an exciting new art center. But this historic mural will stay and be incorporated into the designs for the new center.

properties.title: Cheers from Waco Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Kalyn Dunks, 2022
Spanning on the side of Bicycle World, Dunks' "Cheers from Waco" mural features a collage of people and places that truly do make Waco, Waco. The historic ALICO building, McLennan County Courthouse, Dr. Pepper, and other classic downtown attractions are featured in bright, vibrant colors.

properties.title: City With a Soul (The Findery) Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Shelby Nickel, revised by Kalyn Dunks (2016)
After facing some trademark dispute, The Findery mural, originally reading "Waco Feels Like Home", has since been altered by artist Kalyn Dunks to read "City With a Soul". When searching for a new phrase for the mural, Shelby Pipken found inspiration in a 1915 poem by Herman E. Morris reading "I love a virgin city 'with a soul;' Responsive to an altruistic call; Among her fond admires I'd enroll; And urge a 'Greater' Waco to her goal,". This phrase truly does encompass what Waco is about: spirit, passion, and community.

properties.title: Dichotomy Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Mike Trozzo, 2015
You'll have to head up the stairs at Dichotomy Coffee & Spirits to spot this mural. Make sure you grab your drink first, and you'll be rewarded by one of the best patio views in town!

properties.title: Dr Pepper Museum Mural
properties.description:
"If you feel up to it you should introduce yourself to a stranger today"

This mural, on the wall of the Dr Pepper Museum, draws inspiration from generations of advertising, images, and art that have been associated with the soft drink industry, and the interplay of Americana with the Waco-origin of Dr Pepper. With a color palette referencing historic advertising, the mural evokes the passage of time; time well spent over a drink with friends, bringing people together, which is a common thread for advertising and also for the mural-making process.

 “The making of the mural connected so many people into each other’s lives and I believe its work will continue because of the cumulative energy put forth by everyone at ARTPrenticeship and Creative Waco.”

-Mick Burson, lead designer ARTPrenticeship 2021

THIS PROJECT WAS CREATED BY:
Lead Designer

Mick Burson

Artist Mentors

Christal Peterson, Julie Cervantes and Rajesh Solanki

Film Mentor

Cory Dickman, Mike Hamilton and Justin Sanchez

ARTPrentices

Christopher Ramsey, Jade Moncrief, Jasmin Nunez, Jatorrean Mozee (AV), Johanna Angeles, John Segovia (AV), Juvenal Ramos (AV), Karina Jaramillo-Hernandez, Katie (Trouble) Robinson, Leilani Vences (AV), Marisa Sanchez, Micha Morgan, Yajaira Martinez, Tahlia Tran

Project Manager

Stefanie Wheat Johnson

Intern

Truman Cunningham

properties.title: Each One Reach One Teach One
properties.description:
Artist: Chesley Smith, 2005
One of three murals by Chesley Smith as part of the "Color Us Proud" initiative led by Neighborworks with Cultural Arts of Waco. The murals were designed to bring renewed color, pride and cultural vibrancy to Elm Avenue.

properties.title: Everything's Peachy
properties.description: Mural on the side of Uptown Peach vacation home rental

properties.title: Feast
properties.description: Artist: Ira Watkins

properties.title: Feed Your Body, Feed Your Soul:
properties.description:
Will Suarez, 2019
On the wall of the new Union Hall eatery, this should whisk up interest...especially when the one-way Franklin Aveue becomes a 2-way street. Check out Will's work inside Union Hall in the upstairs area, too.

properties.title: Flower and Line Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Gabby Storey, 2022
Painted on 3 metal doors, this mural adds an element of new life to a beautiful historic building in downtown Waco. The piece features line-work of what may be Magnolia flowers on a dark green background, and is located near Brad Settles' "The Gift", which is a mural that's similar in color scheme and honors Waco's community through the imagery of nature.

properties.title: Georges Mural (Wrap)
properties.description:
Artist: Britain Scago with Bradi Zapata, and David Humphrey, 2019
By George! Famous Georges greet patrons to George's Restaurant. This artwork was generated as part of Baylor Senior Lecturer in Journalism Carol Perry's class. Can you spot country music legend George Strait, boxer George Foreman, comedian George Lopez, and former Presidents George Washington, George H.W. Bush and George W. Bush.

properties.title: Giving Praise: Black Pride
properties.description:
Artist: Chesley Smith, 2005
One of three murals by Chesley Smith as part of the "Color Us Proud" initiative led by Neighborworks with Cultural Arts of Waco. The murals were designed to bring renewed color, pride and cultural vibrancy to Elm Avenue.

properties.title: Heart To Heart (Praetorian Building)
properties.description:
Artists: Barbara Allinbloom and Gregory Semyonoff, 1990

This mural was a gift to Waco from Waco resident Barbara Allinbloom, Russian Cosmonaut Alexandre Tarelkine and renowned Russian artist Gregory Semyonoff. It was the first Soviet American joint art venture during the glasnost era. It represents their joint vision of peace and harmony and commitment to world peace. At the time, the building was owned by Barbara's parents, Fred and Ruth Williams, who were patrons of the project. 

The central image is of Christ expressing his love for the whole world. On the right are American government and church buildings and on the left Russian government and church buildings representing the similarities in the two nations. Both, nations of immigrants from all over the world. The blowing flags depict the reverse side of the flags because the Russian artist felt that this made him feel the tension and destruction the Cold War was blowing across the world. The artists used the old Russian Flag instead of the then current Soviet Red flag with Hammer and Sickle, predicting the collapse of the Soviet Empire that occurred the following year in 1991. 

The inscription written in Russian “только для нас"  and in English “not only for us” points out that the global arms race was destroying the environment and threatening the Christ-centered mission of peace on earth.

It's a reminder to focus on our similarities and appreciate the diversity of all life on this planet and create a future for all to thrive.

Photo Credit, Jerry Larson, Waco Trib

properties.title: Hometown USA
properties.description:
Artist- Elizabeth Downey & Gabby Storey

Hometown USA was designed to bring life, color, and joy to Austin Avenue, a major commute for many Wacoans and tourists. Our hope was to brighten up the ordinary, and create something beautiful for residents and visitors alike to enjoy as they pass through downtown. This mural also allowed emerging artists in Waco to experience the joy of painting, and feel empowered to make Waco more beautiful in the future! We wanted to create a design that would allow those with years of experience, or none at all, to come participate in the arts and discover a love for creating. This project is supported by an American Rescue Plan NEA grant from
Creative Waco. With their support, Hometown USA celebrates a love of bright color and the Waco community with bold Texas spirit.

properties.title: It takes a Village
properties.description:
Artists: Samuel & Kim Torres, 2005
This mural was created as part of the “Color Us Proud” project through NeighborWorks and Doreen Ravenscrof of Waco Cultural Arts Fest. It was based on the phrase, “It takes a village to raise a child,” an Igbo and Yoruba proverb that exists in many African languages. The thought behind the mural was the resurgences of East Waco and its community. We must, as a community, see the potential of every child and cultivate their potential into integral parts of our future.

properties.title: Keep Going
properties.description:
Artist: Will Suarez, assisted by Joe Rooster, 2020

The original idea/message was created and commissioned by Brett Jameson. The wall was previously vandalized and Jameson wanted something to cover it. The design/message is directed to tourist coming into Waco. Though magnolia is great, don’t stop there. Keep going, keep exploring our wonderful city. It’s also directed to Wacoans — to keep pushing, keep growing & keep learning. With the current state of all of our lives during this pandemic, that message seems appropriate for everyone.

properties.title: Lamp of the Body
properties.description:
Artist: Joe Rooster

The eye represents us as people and the colors around it represent life and all that it holds. I wanted something that would grab people's attention and bring a positive energy to the area.

properties.title: Live Oak Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Live Oak Classical School Students, 2019
"oaks of righteousness, the planting of the Lord, that He may be glorified" (Isaiah 61:3). The featured live oak tree mural is symbolic of Live Oak's mission: to bring students closer to God through a classical education. The project was initiated by student Rich Helmer and completed with the assistance of art teacher Bailey Pajela and other Live Oak students.

properties.title: Martin Luther King Jr.
properties.description:
Artist: Ira Watkins, 2005, restored 2019
This mural adorns a historic but now disused bridge footing on the banks of the Brazos River. It was painted to honor the cultural heritage of the historically African American neighborhood on the east side of the river, and its role in the Civil Rights Movement. There is a stairway onto the roof of the bridge footing, and you can climb it to overlook the river.
The mural was commissioned by businessman Donnie Wilkinson and County Commissioner Lester Gibson

properties.title: Metamorphosis
properties.description:
Artist: Will Suarez, 2021
Metamorphosis was inspired by the original art of Kathleen L. Slavin who was a part of Lovely Village and passed January of 2021. She struggled with mental health differences throughout her life and used art to transform her pain. The year she passed, several Lovely Village neighbors were lost and Will Suarez was commissioned to take one of Kathleen’s drawings and transform it into a mural representing a becoming for the many women we lost. It was named 'Metamorphosis.'

properties.title: Montessori Mural
properties.description: Artist: Mick Burson

properties.title: Ode to Oliver
properties.description: Artist: DVLD, 2021

properties.title: OG Customs Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Mick Burson
This piece is painted on the side of OG Customs. The artist asked OG Management for permission to paint the wall that drivers would see as they downtown via Franklin Ave.

“It was a new look,” OG Customs employee Diego Gonzales said. “It pops out, it has a lot color.”

properties.title: Peace Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Chesley Smith, 2005
One of three murals by Chesley Smith as part of the "Color Us Proud" initiative led by Neighborworks with Cultural Arts of Waco. The murals were designed to bring renewed color, pride and cultural vibrancy to Elm Avenue.

properties.title: Point 1
properties.description:
Artist: Mick Burson
On the east wall of Jumpin' John's convenience store, facing 18th St., this mural is filled with colorful and bold geometric shapes and designs.

properties.title: Reaching Beyond Mural
properties.description:
The images in this mural represent the strides humanity has taken when we've had the courage and audacity to reach beyond our limitations into new possibilities of imagination and innovation. On the right is a rocket blasting into uncharted space, as an astronaut surveys the future. The center depicts advancements in energy and mechatronics, with a turbine engine, wind turbines, and motor blueprint. To the left, biology meets technology, as a robotic hand sparks new possibilities in a strand of DNA.

Lead Designer
Natalie Ward

Artist Mentors
Tashita Bibles, Cory Lind and Kristen Thompson

Film Mentors
Cory Dickman, Mike Hamilton and Dominic Villa

ARTPrentices
Victoria Delgado, Natalie Erler, Nayeli Flores, Yajaira Martinez, Sara Olguin, Sylvana Prieto, Star Rosas, Ariel Farley, Mario Perales, Cayleigh Scott, and David Tran

Program Director
Stefanie Wheat-Johnson

Program Manager
Sakina Dixon

Intern
Jade Moncrief

properties.title: Revival Eastside Eatery Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Will Suarez, 2019
Featuring bright blocks of color and shapes, Suarez's mural pays homage to classic Waco historical entities such as Paul Quinn College, mammoths (Waco Mammoth National Monument), 1849 (date Waco was founded), and the state of Texas.

properties.title: St Francis Church Mural
properties.description: Artist:Mick Burson

properties.title: Starry Night
properties.description:
Artist: Jenny Jameson, 2010 
Styled after Van Gogh's famous painting.

properties.title: Storytellers
properties.description:
Nova Acrylic 2013
Artist: David Lowenstein
The East Waco Library Mural was funded by a grant from Mid America Arts Alliance through Cultural Arts of Waco, partner NeighborWorks Waco.  It was designed following a series of community input meetings and was painted with the help of over 60 community members. If you look carefully, you can find all kinds of historic and current “Waco Famous” characters here. The mural is an arts catalyst that impacted the renovation of the East Waco Library as we see it today!

properties.title: The Color of Health Mural
properties.description:
Creative Waco ARTprenticeship Program, 2019

This artwork at Waco Family Health Center, 1911 N. M.L.K. Jr Blvd. was developed through Creative Waco’s ARTPrenticeship program. Aspiring young creatives from Waco ISD high schools earned real world concept-to-completion mentored work experience during the 8-week program. Under guidance from local artist mentors, the apprentices created a fresh and eye-catching work of art that reflects the spirit and beauty of our community.

This artwork combines themes the apprentices drew from hearing community leaders talk about the strong neighborhoods surrounding the Family Health Center. They learned about the history of women who rose up to provide a network of healthcare as midwives, nurses, and  healers, whose life mission became the wellness and flourishing of their neighbors. 

The fruit symbolizes all that we take in or experience that affects our health – individually and together. It has no color of its own. Only through interaction with the woman and the man at her side does it take on color, vibrancy and energy.

“The Color of Health” was created by artist mentors Will Suarez (lead designer), Susan Sistrunk, Bethany Franzen, and Julie Milstead. The apprentices were Julius Merriwether, Magnolia Oliphant, Maria Quinones-Prado, Kaeleana Ramirez, Daphne Rodriguez, Danielle Stevens.

properties.title: The Gift
properties.description:
Artist: Brad Settles, 2021
Modeled after Settles' 2017 painting, "The Gift" serves as a 55 foot homage to Waco's sense of community. The piece, inspired by Cameron Park, features lush greenery and a bow of twine, with no artist signatures. The bow leaves room for interpretation; the "gift" in question may be community, nature, or Waco as a whole. View more background and Settles' work process on Magnolia Network's pilot episode of "Public Artist" (first aired in 2022).

properties.title: The Spirit of We Mural
properties.description:
Creative Waco ARTprenticeship program, 2019
This artwork on the wall of Brotherwell Brewing at 400 E. Bridge St. was developed through Creative Waco’s ARTPrenticeship program. Aspiring young creatives from Waco ISD high schools earned real world concept-to-completion mentored work experience during the 8-week program. Lead Designer was New Orleans Artist, Richard C. Thomas, famous for pioneering “visual jazz”. Under his guidance, and with input from local artist mentors, the apprentices created a work of art that reflects the spirit and beauty of our community.

At first glance, this artwork looks abstract. Like listening to jazz on your front porch, it seems like a jumble of color and rhythm. Then, just like jazz, your attention is rewarded, and the patterns and themes weave their magic.
Look closely, and you will see images and symbols. Families, seeds, animals, birds, plants, earth, air, water and fire; the elements of legend, history, and life. Some symbols come together to create larger patterns and pictures: An African mask (symbol of origin), a whale (symbol that sometimes we find ourselves where we did not choose to be), and a phoenix (symbol of rising from what seems hopeless). The interweaving symbols depict stories the designer and apprentice artists heard from community members as they listened and learned about Bridge Street. Every time you look, you will see a different story emerging. This is “The Spirit of We”.

“The Spirit of We” was created by artist mentors Richard C. Thomas (lead designer and celebrity artist), Shawna Tomes, Cade Kegerreis, and Kristen Thompson. The apprentices were Naya Banda, Treshion Bryant, Brenden Kidd, Fate King, Lillian Olvera and IsaBella Thomas.

properties.title: Tree of Life Mural
properties.description:
The Tree of Life mural represents the unity that is rooted in community, wisdom, and understanding. Individuals grow from their ancestors, passing along knowledge of how they came to be. The many stories are often intertwined when focusing on a specific place, such as historically rich East Waco. This mural is designed to highlight the flourishing community rooted in Elm Avenue.

Generations of families in East Waco have grown and thrived through hardships and represent a vibrant culture that has often been overlooked and under-appreciated. Co-designers Vincent Thomas and Cade Kegerreis considered this project an opportunity to reflect these rich stories and respect the history of this neighborhood while looking to its future and aspirations.

The wall is naturally divided into 7 panels each represents a day of the week, and a facet of the community’s success interwoven with symbolic fruits and stories from East Waco.

Sunday/ Roots of Faith: Sunday celebrates faith, leadership, perseverance, and community love. Religion has always been a way for groups to join for a purpose larger than themselves, sharing in the values of love and acceptance. religion grounds our individual endeavors allowing ideas to connect and flourish into all facets of Life.

THIS PROJECT WAS CREATED BY:
Artist Mentors

Vincent Thomas & Cade Kegerreis (lead designers), Kristen Thompson and Tashita Bibles

Film Mentor

Andreas Zaloumis

ARTPrentices

Jonathan Campos, Vanesa Carvajal, Lina Denson, Rafael Flores, Fate King, Zander Lim, Angelina Monroy, Jasmin Nunez, Lillian Olvera, Larissa Rodriguez, Niala Speedwell, Maria Duarte Tavera, Tahlia Tran, and Ja’Nasia Whitfield

Project Manager

Stefanie Wheat Johnson

properties.title: Visiting Angels Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Kalyn Dunks
Dunks' mural on a Visting Angels senior care facility reads: "do not forget to show hospitality to strangers, for by so doing some have shown hospitality to angels, Hebrews 13:2". Visiting Angels provides homes for the elderly on a national scale, and this mural serves as a reminder to treat others, especially strangers or elderly, with hospitality. The piece is highly interactive and encourages viewers to take photos as an "angel" in front of the painted halo and wings.

properties.title: Waco Y'all Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Gabby Storey, 2022
Right on the corner of Fuzzy's Taco Shop, Storey's "Waco Y'all" mural absolutely POPS with vibrant colors and abstract shapes. The mural beams with texas-hometown pride.

properties.title: Wacotown
properties.description:
Artist: Wacotown collective, 2012
With the advent of Fabled Bookstore, these windows have now been reopened, but before that, this mural started the ball rolling on a new arts-based vision for Waco. The right hand side was originally a count-down to the first game in the brand new McLane Stadium.

properties.title: Wildflowers I
properties.description: Artist: ARTPrenticeship Team 2024

properties.title: Wildflowers II
properties.description: ARTPrenticeship Team 2024

properties.title: You Look Nice Today Wacotown
properties.description:
Artist: Binty Bint, 2014
You Look Nice Today Wacotown
British artist Binty Bint painted this while on a trip to Texas in 2014. It's on 115 N. 7th St. Visible from Washington Ave, and on the backside of the building.

properties.title: Freedom Fountain
properties.description:
1973, restored by Roth Construction, 2014
Waco's Freedom Fountain was built in 1973 to commemorate 12 Waco residents who traveled to Paris in 1971 to negotiate with North Vietnamese officials for the release of U.S. prisoners of war. The Freedom Fountain stands as a monument for Freedom for past and present war veterans. The fountain includes cast concrete panels with the phrase "FREEDOM" in 54 different languages. 
photo credit: Richard Duhrkopf

properties.title: Kate Friend Animal Fountain
properties.description:
1909, restored 1983 and 2018
This fountain was originally presented to the City of Waco by the National Humane Alliance in 1909 in honor of Kate Friend, who worked with the Waco Humane Society for over 40 years and was credited with founding Waco's first animal shelter. 
The fountain was designed to provide water and relief to animals of all sizes, with a large basin on the top for horses and mules and four bowls at the base for dogs and other smaller animals.

properties.title: Sturgis Fountain
properties.description:
2005
The Carroll and Francis Sturgis Fountain, is named in honor of Carroll W Sturgis and his wife Frances Burnett Sturgis by Keep Waco Beautiful on its 25th Anniversary. Frances founded the Greater Waco Beautification Association (which later became KWB). She also served as a leader of Junior League, the Symphony Council, Art Center of Waco, Brazos River Festival, United Waco, Historic Waco Foundation and was a City Council member.

properties.title: Blek Le Rat "Chopin"
properties.description:
Blek le Rat, November 2018
Parisian artist “Blek-le-Rat", born Xavier Prou, pioneered the use of layered stencil spray-paint techniques to achieve speed, stealth, and artistic vibrancy. His technique became the “go to” method for a generation of iconic street artists, including the infamous (and still technically anonymous) British artist, “Banksy” (of auction-shredding renown). 

This piece is on the wall of Waco Running Company, facing Union Hall (Food Hall). Is is just us, or does Chopin look like he's just been for a run and now he's hungry?

properties.title: Blek Le Rat 'Violinist'
properties.description:
Blek le Rat, November 2018
Parisian artist “Blek-le-Rat", born Xavier Prou, pioneered the use of layered stencil spray-paint techniques to achieve speed, stealth, and artistic vibrancy. His technique became the “go to” method for a generation of iconic street artists, including the infamous (and still technically anonymous) British artist, “Banksy” (of auction-shredding renown). 

Many of Prou's artworks depict the virtuosity of other artforms such as dance and music. This violinist watches over the patrons of Dichotomy Coffee & Spirits, at 508 Austin Avenue. Check out the interior and the roof patio, too, you'll find more art, entertainment, and refreshment for every palate.

properties.title: Blek Le Rat 'Young Picasso'
properties.description:
Blek le Rat, November 2018
Parisian artist “Blek-le-Rat", born as Xavier Prou, pioneered the use of layered stencil spray-paint techniques to achieve speed, stealth, and artistic vibrancy. His technique became the “go to” method for a generation of iconic street artists, including the infamous (and still technically anonymous) British artist, “Banksy” (of auction-shredding renown).
This mural depicts the young Picasso, and is on the wall of the new location of the Art Center of Waco. Follow the link to see what masterpieces are in the works at this exciting new facility.

properties.title: Blek Le Rat 'Victor Hugo'
properties.description:
Blek le Rat, November 2018
Parisian artist “Blek-le-Rat", born Xavier Prou, pioneered the use of layered stencil spray-paint techniques to achieve speed, stealth, and artistic vibrancy. His technique became the “go to” method for a generation of iconic street artists, including the infamous (and still technically anonymous) British artist, “Banksy” (of auction-shredding renown). 

When Prou visited Waco, he made time to learn about the community. Brotherwell Brewing, where this mural is found, had just opened, and its owners were passionate about creating a place for people of all kinds to gather, think and drink. Prou was inspired to place Victor Hugo here, as a character who was often inspired by rubbing shoulders with artisans over a pint of beer. Since this photo was taken, Brotherwell Brewing has become home to another major mural, and a vibrant program that ranges from Shakespeare readings to artisanal markets and live music events.

properties.title: Baby Giraffe
properties.description:
Artist: Tom Tischler
A bronze sculpture of a baby giraffe, laying comfortably on the ground. 
Located inside Cameron Park Zoo; Africa walkway across from African hoofstock yard.

properties.title: Beaver
properties.description:
Artist: Eugenia Kamrath "GiGi" Mygdal
A bronze sculpture of a beaver bitting a piece of wood that will be used for the beavers home.
Located inside Cameron Park Zoo; Brazos River Country Aviary.

properties.title: Cranes
properties.description:
A bronze sculpture of two cranes enjoying each others company.
Located inside Cameron Park Zoo; Outside Brazos River Country Aviary near alligators.

properties.title: Galapagos Tortoise
properties.description:
Artist: Tom Tischler
Bronze sculpture of a Galapagos Tortoise standing tall, keeping its head held high.
Located inside Cameron Park Zoo; zoo meadows near playground.

properties.title: Komodo Dragon
properties.description:
Artist: Tom Tischler
A bronze sculpture of a Komodo Dragon, which are the largest, heaviest lizards in the world and one of the few with a venomous bite.
Located inside Cameron Park Zoo; Asian Forest deck across from Komodo Habitat.

properties.title: Sea Turtles
properties.description:
Artist: Eric Tischler
A bronze sculpture of two sea turtles swimming through coral in the sea. This sculpture is dedicated to Josh Risinger, who will always be remembered by his friends and family for his quick-wit and fun-loving personality.
Located inside Cameron Park Zoo; inside marine aquarium.

properties.title: Orangutan
properties.description:
Artist: Tom Tischler
A bronze sculpture of the largest arboreal mammal known as the Orangutan sitting peacefully on the floor.
Located inside Cameron Park Zoo; Asian Forest play area.

properties.title: Otters
properties.description:
Artist: Tom Tischler
A bronze sculpture of two otters just swimming and playing in the water.
Located inside Cameron Park Zoo; Brazos River Country otter exhibit.

properties.title: Spilling the Wind
properties.description:
By David Turner
Silicon bronze

Since Roman times, the eagle in art has been a symbol of power and clear vision. Here the artist has placed the eagle in an almost vertical soaring motion. One wing reaches heavenward and the other points down to earth. Meanwhile, those sharp eyes are looking ahead – perhaps to the future. Notice the color variation, which is due to the application of a special patina that defines the feathers and helps protect the bronze.

properties.title: Roxy
properties.description:
By John A. Maisano

“Roxy is sassy and playful, and she grabs so much attention wherever she is displayed. Her pose was inspired by a fox I saw leaning back, scratching, with her head slightly tilted in an almost curious way. I loved interpreting this pose with strong sharp angles moving into flowing curves. As I sculpted the front legs in a playful crossed position, to my surprise, a heart formed on the chest. Very often a heart forms in my work as I am sculpting, and it has now become a signature in so many of my pieces.

People always have fox stories, which is what really attracted me to this creature as a subject to sculpt. My hope is that Roxy will bring joy and smiles to everyone who sees her and experiences this sassy little fox.” - Maisano

properties.title: The Kids
properties.description:
By Lori Hough
Bronze Sculpture

This piece captures the natural play that is part of how young orangutans (and young humans) learn how to be a successful part of a family unit. Like us, these animals have complex social emotions and interactions, curiosity about the world around them, and a sense of humor. What do you think these two young orangutans are doing? What will happen next?

properties.title: Stalking Fox
properties.description:
By David Turner
cast in silicon bronze

Notice how alert and stealthy this animal looks. This artist often chooses to put the animals he creates in action poses. What do you think that tells us about the animal? In European and East Asian folklore, foxes are often a symbol for cleverness, stealth, or trickery. Do you know what it means to “outfox” someone?

properties.title: Trouble
properties.description:
By Bob Guelich
Plinth by Connie Brenner and Wendy Carter

Bears are symbolic animals in many art traditions, including Native American folklore. Their ability to walk on two legs made them seem closely linked with people, and their ability to fight even when injured made them symbols of strength, determination and healing.

properties.title: Bats Taking Flight
properties.description:
By Bryant J. Stanton

Bats Taking Flight, plays on the wind moving the silhouetted bats about on long sinuous tubes of aluminum striking neighboring tubes, and generating ‘tinny-clanking’ sounds.

The silhouetted bats are painted with a reflective, deep-blue color, representing bats at dusk. The reflective paint picks up on ambient light, headlights of passing cars, and beams from flashlights, all bouncing off of the painted bats and reeds. The bouncing, reflected light, contrasted against the dark park surroundings, appear as illuminated bats flying about.

properties.title: Mother and Child
properties.description:
By Dan D. Brook
cast bronze

While the artist was creating this work, his daughter-in-law developed cancer. Unsurprisingly, it was impossible for Brook to focus on his artwork with a life and death crisis facing his beloved family. After his daughter-in-law started to recover, he resumed work on the piece, pouring into this sculpture some of the emotional complexity of this experience. Notice how the parent arcs protectively over her chick…but they are not touching.

properties.title: Ornate Spiny-Tailed Lizard
properties.description:
By Solomon Bassoff and Domenica Mottarella
Steel and pigmented concrete with glass mosaic tile

Notice how the sun catches the iridescent tiles on the lizard’s back and makes them look like every color in the rainbow. One of the ways you can spot the work of these artists is the mesmerizing eyes that their sculptures have, which are made specially out of glass for each character. Do you see that this lizard is smiling?

properties.title: Gerenuk
properties.description:
By Kent Ullberg
Broonze sculpture

Ullberg spent 7 years in Africa studying, and chose this piece because he found the gerenuk to be an exotic and interesting antelope. He found it exciting to depict him reaching up, standing on his skinny back legs, which challenged the stretched integrity of the composition. Because of this, Ullberg had to include special engineering tricks such as embedding stainless steel structures in the bronze.

properties.title: Wise Elephant
properties.description:
By Trevor O’Tool
Cast bronze

The elephant is anthropomorphized, meaning that O’Tool gave him human characteristics. It is actually a humorous interpretation of Rodin’s famous sculpture, “The Thinker”. The artwork presents a unique opportunity to see just how massive elephants are, by sitting right beside one!

properties.title: Whitetail
properties.description:
By Bob Guelich
Bronze sculpture

Deer are among the very first animals to have been represented in early human art. They can be found in the earliest cave paintings and carvings ever discovered. Our relationship with deer is that of hunter and hunted, but it’s also more complex than that. Deer can also represent the soul, a spirit of mystery, and are often symbolic of shape-shifting deities. They play a sacred role in Indian, Egyptian, Greek, Huichol (Mexican), Hindu, Celtic, Buddhist, and Islamic cultures. In Japanese Shinto tradition, deer are kept at shrines as sacred messengers to the gods.

properties.title: Box Turtle II
properties.description:
By David Turner
Silicon bronze

Turtles are another animal that features widely in traditional art and mythology. In India, and among east coast Native American tribes, such as the Iroquois and Lenape, a giant turtle or tortoise is part of their creation story, carrying the Earth on its back. In cultures where turtles are traditionally eaten, their shells are often used as bowls and are decorated or carved. Notice the detail on this turtle’s shell and the expression on its face. What do you think the artist is expressing about this ancient creature?

properties.title: American Alligator
properties.description:
By David Turner
Silicon bronze & patina

American Alligator was cast in silicon bronze right in the artist’s personal foundry, which he has operated with his father since 1983.The variation in colors on this artwork was achieved through the formation of patina, which is caused by the reaction of some metal surfaces to chemicals. The process can occur naturally over time, or it can be achieved more quickly by reheating the metal and applying chemicals manually.

properties.title: Sumatran Tiger
properties.description:
By Tom Tischler
bronze sculpture

He spent a lot of time observing animals and their natural behavior through his conservation work, and his wife is a zoo veterinarian, so he gets plenty of opportunity to see them up close, too. He hopes that his art will stimulate a greater awareness of, interest in, and appreciation for these animals, and the important work of Cameron Park Zoo.

properties.title: Night Cat
properties.description:
By Douwe Blumberg

Night Cat is a classic bronze sculpture cast in standard art-quality silicon bronze and finished with a classic ferric nitrate hot patina. Notice the cat’s stealthy posture–Is it carefully taking a drink along the exposed riverbank, or is it stalking its next prey?

properties.title: Giraffe
properties.description:
By Solomon Bassoff and Domenica Mottarella

Giraffe is made from pigmented concrete, and weights almost 1000 pounds! Take a close look at its eyes. They are made from hand-blown glass. Notice that if you watch this sculpture for a while, you will see people of all ages and even animals walk up to interact with it as if it were alive. Why do you think this sculpture invites this kind of interaction?

properties.title: Lioness with Cubs
properties.description:
By Tom Tischler
Bronze sculpture with patina 

“These creatures are not so different from ourselves, we share a lot in common. Just like us, mother animals love their babies and the babies love their mothers.” - Tischler

This piece has an incredible amount of detail informed by how well the artist knows his subjects. Notice how each cub has a different personality. Notice, too, that although this is a bronze sculpture, the patina is designed to give a realistic impression of animals whose coloring is designed to blend with the African grassland where they live.

properties.title: Hearts Unfolding
properties.description:
By John A. Maisano

Maisano chose the Heron because of its graceful lines and decided on a powerful, upward-looking pose. He loved how the wings looked like a heart as he began sculpting them, thus the name “Hearts Unfolding.”

properties.title: Ely Playing with a Stick
properties.description:
By Meg White
Bronze replica of an original work in limestone.

Ely was an unusually large calf, and his front legs had been so restricted in the womb that he was born unable to walk. It was feared he would die, as he had trouble nursing, but thanks to the care of his mother, and his determined attempts to straighten his legs, he gained strength, eventually overcoming his disability, and is today a fully-grown bull elephant in Kenya’s Amboseli National Park.

In this piece Ely has his legs fully stretched out in play. The original artwork was carved in limestone using various power tools–large and small angle grinders, a hammer drill and various burrs with a die grinder gave the final texture of the many wrinkles of an elephant’s skin

properties.title: Taking Flight
properties.description:
By Douwe Blumberg
Cast in an aluminum/magnesium alloy known as “Almag 535.”

Blumberg wanted to create a Waco-specific sculpture of birds taking off that would complement the character of the location. He wanted to interact with the themes of hope and optimism captured in the mural of the origami cranes taking flight (on Jackson & University Parks in Downtown Waco).

The bird shape he has designed around is styled on geese, which are also evocative symbols of spirituality and hope in Native American and Celtic cultures. The artist has created a dramatic upward sweep, capturing the idea of a flock (or community) taking off together.

properties.title: Meerkat Tribe
properties.description:
By Tom Tischler
The base for this sculpture was created by Waco-based concrete artist, Victor All in Blome, and was constructed to look like the termite mounds where meerkats often build their burrows.

properties.title: Geri
properties.description:
By Robbie Barber
Abstracted but life-size giraffe made from tubular stainless steel crafted from stainless steel pipe in three different diameters: 3”, 2.5”, and 2”). 

He is approximately 18’ tall, with its neck extended and its tongue reaching for the canopy of live oak leaves that frames its site in Pecan Bottoms.

properties.title: Tyson
properties.description:
By Solomon Bassoff and Domenica Mottarella
Steel and sculpted, pigmented concrete. It is a much larger than life piece that weighs in at 1163lbs.

The artwork is named for Paul Leighton Tyson (1886-1950) who was Waco High School’s championship-winning football coach from 1913-1942, and is revered as one of the most successful high school football coaches of all time. Under Tyson’s leadership, Waco High played six consecutive national championship games between 1922 and 1927, losing only two. 

The sculpture evokes the original Waco High School tiger mascot. If you know someone who was a Waco High student in those glory years, ask what the tiger mascot means to them. Notice that the artist has captured the characteristics of a real tiger as well as the characteristics of a mascot tiger. What do you think makes us choose an animal like a tiger to represent a sports team?

properties.title: White Rhino
properties.description:
By Solomon Bassoff and Domenica Mottarella
Steel and sculpted, pigmented concrete. weighing 1153lbs.

Notice how solid this animal is. It’s almost like an animal version of a tank. But sadly, its bulk is one of the reasons rhinos are so endangered. They are not as nimble or adaptable to hunting or habitat changes as some animals. Notice how careful the artist has been to capture all the details of this increasingly rare animal. What stands out most to you?

properties.title: African Lion
properties.description:
By Solomon Bassoff and Domenica Mottarella
Steel and sculpted, pigmented concrete. It is 100” long, and 83” tall and weighs 1076 pounds.

Like all Faducci’s sculptures, notice how the artist captures real personality in this animal. Lions have a strong tradition in western art as heraldic symbols of power, strength and royalty. Why do you think humans see these qualities in a lion? What characteristics do you see in this particular lion? What animal would you choose to represent your personality?

properties.title: Afro Pach-Patch
properties.description:
By Andrew Scott
Mother & baby African Elephants
Open wire frame construction composed of 3/8” steel rods with a 6-layer powder coated surface.

Human interaction with elephants has been part of their story – and ours – for thousands of years. Despite their strength, elephants submit themselves to human training and are major economic assets in many places, performing work that would take the strength of dozens of humans.

Notice how although these sculptures represent solid and bulky animals that we characterize as strong and resilient, these linear abstractions are really subtle. They look fragile, almost look like ghosts of elephants walking across the park. Why do you think the artist has depicted a mother and baby elephant like this? What does it make you think about these animals? What does it make you think about their relationship with humans?

properties.title: Razak and Auntie
properties.description:
Solomon Bassoff and Domenica Mottarella
Orangutans
Steel and sculpted, pigmented concrete. It is 64” wide and 65” high.

Razak and Auntie depicts two orangutans that live in Cameron Park Zoo; Razak, who was born in January 2017, and Kutai, his “auntie”. Just after Razak was born, his mother, Mei, began to display irregular, threatening behavior, and the decision was made to remove Razak from her care for his protection. Zoo staff decided to take over most of the infant’s daily care in hopes Mei could eventually raise him on her own. 

During this time, Razak was introduced to Kutai, so that she could provide surrogate care and all-important species bonding for him. Later, Mei recovered from what turned out to be a form of post-partum depression. She and Razak were reintroduced and they now have a strong mother-child relationship.

properties.title: Texas Ranger
properties.description:
Don Hunt
Bronze Sculpture, 2008

properties.title: Major George B. Erath--Frontiersman
properties.description:
Robert Summers
Bronze Sculpture, 1976

properties.title: Blek Le Rat: 'In Honor of Kurt Kaiser'
properties.description:
Blek le Rat, November 2018
Parisian artist “Blek-le-Rat", born as Xavier Prou, pioneered the use of layered stencil spray-paint techniques to achieve speed, stealth, and artistic vibrancy. His technique became the “go to” method for a generation of iconic street artists, including the infamous (and still technically anonymous) British artist, “Banksy” (of auction-shredding renown). When Prou visited Waco to paint this series of six murals in November 2018, he arrived the day that beloved Waco-based musician and composer Kurt Kaiser passed away. Learning of Kurt's role in inspiring a generation of musicians, he wanted this piece to honor Kurt, whom he never met, but felt a creative kinship with.

properties.title: Blek Le Rat 'Selfie Rat'
properties.description:
Blek le Rat, November 2018
Parisian artist “Blek-le-Rat", born as Xavier Prou, pioneered the use of layered stencil spray-paint techniques to achieve speed, stealth, and artistic vibrancy. His technique became the “go to” method for a generation of iconic street artists, including the infamous (and still technically anonymous) British artist, “Banksy” (of auction-shredding renown). 

The trademark rat character (which Banksy uses, too) is an anagram of "art". Sometimes rats are more numerous than the people in a city, but they hide where they are hard to detect and come out in secret - just like street artists. 

This little mural requires you to go deeper into the artworld of Waco to find it. You'll need to ask at the front desk of Cultivate 7twelve gallery, 712 Austin Avenue.

properties.title: Cactus Rose Mural
properties.description:
Artist: Allie Menchaca, 2021
Spanning across the side of a western store called Cactus Rose, Menchaca's mural features a classic Texas-western scene. Along with a lone cowboy and horse are clumps of various cacti, blending into an explosion of pink roses.

properties.title: Clasé Mural #2
properties.description: Artist: Mick Burson, 2019

properties.title: Enve (Now Covered)
properties.description:
Artist: Skcoobavelli Trevino
This mural depicts actor Johnny Depp playing the role of Edward Scissorhands from the 1990 movie directed by Tim Burton. It adorns the side of Enve beauty bar.

properties.title: Glenn, answer your phone.
properties.description:
Artist: Mick Burson, 2020

The idea behind this mural was inspired by a metaphor in the movie Field of Dreams.

This was the beginning of the pandemic and I was thinking a lot about that phrase “You can lead a horse to water but you cant make it drink”. I just kept wishing the horse would take the advice. I was open to recommendations and I was able to understand how important advice and being still is. Also Texas references resurface in Burson's work and he indulge in country themes for a bit. 

So these two aligned and there are horses drinking water, there is nature and references to its blissfully unaware beauty, there is references to human spaces where a viewer can feel more connected and hopefully investigate further. There are moments that make no sense at all and those are beautiful and the hardest to make, and also the hardest to not cover up in a moment of insecurity. It is a visual journey that speeds up, slows down, and sometimes stands still.

properties.title: Images Past - South Waco
properties.description:
Nova Acrylic 2019
Lead Artists: Ira Watkins & Chesley Smith, 2019
This is Waco’s largest mural to date 300ft x 16ft. It was commissioned by Diversified Product Development in partnership with Cultural Arts of Waco, and with a grant from Creative Waco's Arts Match Program. This mural depicts scenes from South Waco’s history. Features the Folk artist style of Ira Watkins and the Contemporary style of Chesley Smith, together with six additional Waco artists and over 40 community volunteers creating a series of images of the past.

properties.title: Muse Status
properties.description:
Artist: Brooke C Collins, 2019

Collins' muse was an imaginary 1980’s plant and neon covered landscape. “Miss TV” is leading the way and showing off her fashion. Clasé is owned by a young local "big thinker" and she wanted the wall to match that energy. She hopes it can bring some fun to all the trailblazers in Waco.

properties.title: Shephard's Heart Pantry Mural
properties.description:
Artist, Mick Burson
This mural is located on the side of the Shephard’s Heart Food Pantry where artist Mick Burson’s mother worked.

“I think it’s one of my favorite pieces in Waco,” Burson says. “I really like the time in my life that it happened, it was definitely the whole experience of painting the wall. I also think the gradients are really cool. It looks like its part of it rather than on top of it.”

properties.title: Stone Hearth Mural (Covered)
properties.description:
Tony Bryant, 2017
This Indian style mandala design was commissioned by Roshan and Rima Thakor, husband and wife owners of Stone Hearth Indian Cafe. There was also a hand mural inside the restaurant, however, both have now been covered, as Stone Hearth is no longer at this location.
